mechanism: Perception of action generates inference of mental states. Observer identifies target's action at multiple levels (low-level details vs. high-level consequences), with higher-level identification associated with attribution of intentionality, goals, and cognition. Liking mediates both identification and mind attribution through perspective-taking mechanism.
domain: social psychology, action perception, theory of mind

mechanism: Self-control exertion depletes glucose from bloodstream, reducing available metabolic energy. Low blood glucose impairs subsequent self-control performance. Acts of self-control reduce glucose levels; low post-task glucose predicts poor performance on next self-control task; glucose restoration (via drink) eliminates performance impairment.

Actor weights introspective information (thoughts, intentions) above behavioral evidence when assessing own conformity, while observers weight behavior equally or more. This introspection illusion causes systematic underestimation of own susceptibility to social influence relative to others' susceptibility.

Cognitive dissonance reduction via approach-oriented motivational processes. Following decision commitment, left frontal cortical activation increases to support action implementation. Modulating this activation affects degree of attitude change (spreading of alternatives).

Victim-perpetrator conflict creates asymmetrical resource deprivation: victims lose sense of power and status, perpetrators lose moral image and social acceptance. Satisfying these differential emotional needs via empowerment (for victims) and acceptance (for perpetrators) restores psychological resources and facilitates reconciliation.
domain: social psychology, conflict resolution, interpersonal processes

mechanism: Awareness of meaningful endings amplifies mixed emotional experience (happiness plus sadness). Time constraints and perception of finitude focus attention on emotionally significant aspects while simultaneously highlighting transience, creating simultaneous positive and negative affect.
